user_query: I want to analyze how the signal quality varies across different atmospheric conditions. For each condition, give condition name, the average quality value, the median quality value, and count how many usable signals there are. Sort the result by average value.
sol_sql:
/* 
Intent: Analyze signal quality (SNQI) with atmospheric conditions using window functions
Knowledge Used: SNQI (id:0)
Advanced Features: Window functions, conditional aggregation
*/
WITH signal_quality AS (
    -- Step 1: Calculate SNQI for all signals with their observation conditions
    SELECT 
        s.SignalRegistry,
        s.SnrRatio - 0.1 * ABS(s.NoiseFloorDbm) AS SNQI,
        o.WeathProfile
    FROM Signals s
    JOIN Telescopes t ON s.TelescRef = t.TelescRegistry
    JOIN Observatories o ON t.ObservStation = o.ObservStation
)
-- Step 2: Rank signals by quality within weather profiles
SELECT 
    WeathProfile,
    AVG(SNQI) AS avg_snqi,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY SNQI) AS median_snqi,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE SNQI > 0) AS analyzable_signals
FROM signal_quality
GROUP BY WeathProfile
ORDER BY avg_snqi DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/*
Intent: Analyze Atmospheric Observability Index (AOI) with weather conditions
Knowledge Used: AOI (id:1)
Advanced Features: Window functions, conditional aggregation
*/

WITH observ_conditions AS (
    -- Step 1: Calculate AOI for all signals with their observation conditions
    SELECT 
        s.SignalRegistry,
        o.AtmosTransparency * (1 - o.HumidityRate / 100) * (1 - 0.02 * o.WindSpeedMs) AS AOI,
        o.WeathProfile
    FROM 
        Signals s
    JOIN 
        Telescopes t ON s.TelescRef = t.TelescRegistry
    JOIN 
        Observatories o ON t.ObservStation = o.ObservStation
)

-- Step 2: Aggregate AOI metrics by weather profile
SELECT 
    WeathProfile,
    AVG(AOI) AS avg_aoi,
    PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY AOI) AS median_aoi,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE AOI > 0) AS valid_observations
FROM 
    observ_conditions
GROUP BY 
    WeathProfile
ORDER BY 
    avg_aoi DESC;

user_query: List the top 5 high-risk data flows, showing each flows ID, risk score, and data sensitivity. Sort by data sensitivity.
sol_sql:
WITH HighRiskFlows AS (
    SELECT 
        df.RecordRegistry, 
        COALESCE(rm.RiskAssess / NULLIF(rm.CtrlEff, 0), 0) AS res, 
        COALESCE(dp.VolGB, 0) * 
            CASE 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'High' THEN 3 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'Medium' THEN 2 
                ELSE 1 
            END AS dsi
    FROM DataFlow df
    LEFT JOIN RiskManagement rm 
        ON df.RecordRegistry = rm.FlowLink
    LEFT JOIN DataProfile dp 
        ON df.RecordRegistry = dp.FlowSign
    WHERE COALESCE(rm.RiskAssess / NULLIF(rm.CtrlEff, 0), 0) > 0.7 
        AND COALESCE(dp.VolGB, 0) * 
            CASE 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'High' THEN 3 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'Medium' THEN 2 
                ELSE 1 
            END > 100
),
RankedFlows AS (
    SELECT 
        RecordRegistry, 
        res, 
        dsi, 
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY dsi DESC) AS volume_rank
    FROM HighRiskFlows
)
SELECT 
    RecordRegistry, 
    ROUND(res::numeric, 2) AS risk_exposure_score, 
    ROUND(dsi::numeric, 2) AS data_sensitivity_index
FROM RankedFlows
WHERE volume_rank <= 5
ORDER BY volume_rank
LIMIT 5;
follow_up_sol_sql:
WITH HighRiskFlows AS (
    SELECT 
        df.RecordRegistry, 
        COALESCE(rm.RiskAssess / NULLIF(rm.CtrlEff, 0), 0) AS res, 
        COALESCE(dp.VolGB, 0) * 
            CASE 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'High' THEN 3 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'Medium' THEN 2 
                ELSE 1 
            END AS dsi
    FROM DataFlow df
    LEFT JOIN RiskManagement rm 
        ON df.RecordRegistry = rm.FlowLink
    LEFT JOIN DataProfile dp 
        ON df.RecordRegistry = dp.FlowSign
    WHERE COALESCE(rm.RiskAssess / NULLIF(rm.CtrlEff, 0), 0) > 1.0 
        AND COALESCE(dp.VolGB, 0) * 
            CASE 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'High' THEN 3 
                WHEN dp.DataSense = 'Medium' THEN 2 
                ELSE 1 
            END > 100
        AND df.OrigNation != df.DestNation
),
RankedFlows AS (
    SELECT 
        RecordRegistry, 
        res, 
        dsi, 
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY dsi DESC) AS volume_rank
    FROM HighRiskFlows
)
SELECT 
    RecordRegistry, 
    ROUND(res::numeric, 2) AS risk_exposure_score, 
    ROUND(dsi::numeric, 2) AS data_sensitivity_index
FROM RankedFlows
WHERE volume_rank <= 5
ORDER BY volume_rank
LIMIT 5;

user_query: For our polar safety assessment, I need to evaluate the safety equipment's reliability. Please provide a report showing the equipment code, equipment type, life support status, reliability score, reliability classification. Also include a JSON summary of oxygen status , medical status, and safety system status as support systems status. Focus only on safety equipment and sort the results.
sol_sql:
-- Intent: Calculate Life Support System Reliability (LSSR) for critical equipment
-- Uses knowledge IDs 33, 1, 51 and 6 with advanced JSON processing

-- Step 1: Calculate ORS (Operational Readiness Score) for equipment
WITH ors_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        om.opmainteqref,
        CASE 
            WHEN om.operationalstatus = 'Active' THEN 10 * (1 - om.operationhours / om.maintenancecyclehours)
            WHEN om.operationalstatus = 'Standby' THEN 5 * (1 - om.operationhours / om.maintenancecyclehours)
            ELSE 0 
        END AS ors
    FROM operationmaintenance om
),
-- Step 2: Calculate TIE (Thermal Insulation Efficiency) for each structure
tie_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        thermaleqref,
        CASE 
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Good' THEN 0.9 - (heatlossratekwh / 10)
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Fair' THEN 0.6 - (heatlossratekwh / 10)
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Poor' THEN 0.3 - (heatlossratekwh / 10)
            ELSE 0
        END AS tie
    FROM thermalsolarwindandgrid
),
-- Step 3: Calculate LSSR (Life Support System Reliability) using the formula
-- LSSR = 0.7 * ORS + 0.3 * TIE
lssr_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        oc.opmainteqref,
        0.7 * oc.ors + 0.3 * COALESCE(tc.tie, 0) AS lssr
    FROM ors_calc oc
    LEFT JOIN tie_calc tc ON oc.opmainteqref = tc.thermaleqref
)
-- Step 4: Analyze life support systems reliability
SELECT 
    e.equipmentcode,
    e.equipmenttype,
    ls.lifesupportstatus,
    ROUND(lc.lssr::numeric, 2) AS lssr,
    CASE 
        WHEN lc.lssr >= 0.8 THEN 'High Reliability'
        WHEN lc.lssr >= 0.6 THEN 'Moderate Reliability'
        ELSE 'Low Reliability' 
    END AS reliability_classification,
    jsonb_build_object(
        'oxygen_status', ls.oxygensupplystatus,
        'medical_status', ls.medicalequipmentstatus,
        'safety_system_status', ls.safetysystemstatus
    ) AS support_systems_status
FROM equipment e
JOIN lssr_calc lc ON e.equipmentcode = lc.opmainteqref
JOIN lightingandsafety ls ON e.equipmentcode = ls.lighteqref
WHERE e.equipmenttype = 'Safety'
ORDER BY lssr DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/*  Aggregate counts and statistics for Life-Support System Reliability classes
    ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      • Builds on the ORS, TIE → LSSR computation (KB IDs 1, 6, 33)
      • Applies the LSRC bands (KB ID 51)
*/
WITH ors_calc AS (                       -- Operational Readiness Score (KB 1)
    SELECT
        om.opmainteqref,
        CASE
            WHEN om.operationalstatus = 'Active'  THEN 10 * (1 - om.operationhours / om.maintenancecyclehours)
            WHEN om.operationalstatus = 'Standby' THEN  5 * (1 - om.operationhours / om.maintenancecyclehours)
            ELSE 0
        END AS ors
    FROM operationmaintenance AS om
),
tie_calc AS (                            -- Thermal Insulation Efficiency (KB 6)
    SELECT
        thermaleqref,
        CASE
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Good' THEN 0.9 - heatlossratekwh / 10
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Fair' THEN 0.6 - heatlossratekwh / 10
            WHEN insulationstatus = 'Poor' THEN 0.3 - heatlossratekwh / 10
            ELSE 0
        END AS tie
    FROM thermalsolarwindandgrid
),
lssr_values AS (                         -- Life-Support System Reliability (KB 33)
    SELECT
        e.equipmentcode,
        0.7 * oc.ors + 0.3 * COALESCE(tc.tie, 0) AS lssr
    FROM equipment                 AS e
    JOIN ors_calc                  AS oc ON e.equipmentcode = oc.opmainteqref
    LEFT JOIN tie_calc             AS tc ON e.equipmentcode = tc.thermaleqref
    WHERE e.equipmenttype = 'Safety'
)
SELECT                                    -- Aggregate by LSRC band (KB 51)
    CASE
        WHEN lssr >= 0.8 THEN 'High Reliability'
        WHEN lssr >= 0.6 THEN 'Moderate Reliability'
        ELSE              'Low Reliability'
    END                                            AS reliability_classification,
    COUNT(*)                                       AS equipment_count,
    ROUND(AVG(lssr)::numeric, 2)                   AS avg_lssr,
    ROUND(MIN(lssr)::numeric, 2)                   AS min_lssr,
    ROUND(MAX(lssr)::numeric, 2)                   AS max_lssr
FROM lssr_values
GROUP BY reliability_classification
ORDER BY reliability_classification;

user_query: I want to analyze the RGB quality of various gaming peripherals and classify them according to the RGB Quality system. Please provide each device's manufacturer, model, device type, RGB brightness, color accuracy, number of RGB zones, RGB modes, supported colors, and their RGB implementation quality score and classification. Sort them by RGB quality score from highest to lowest.
sol_sql:
/*
Analysis Goal: Analyze RGB lighting quality and implementation across gaming peripherals
Knowledge Used: ID 6 - RGB Implementation Quality, ID 51
Advanced Features: JSON functions, LATERAL JOIN, conditional aggregation
*/

-- Step 1: Calculate RGB Implementation Quality (RIQ)
WITH rgb_analysis AS (
    SELECT 
        r.rgbregistry,
        d.makername,
        d.modnum,
        t.devscope,
        r.rgbbright,
        r.rgbcoloracc,
        r.rgbzones,
        r.rgbmodes,
        r.rgbcolors,        -- Calculate RIQ based on formula from knowledge ID 6
        (r.rgbbright / 100.0) * (r.rgbcoloracc / 10.0) * (0.5 + r.rgbzones / 20.0) AS riq
    FROM rgb r
    JOIN mechanical m ON r.rgbmechref = m.mechregistry
    JOIN deviceidentity d ON m.mechdevref = d.devregistry
    JOIN testsessions t ON d.devsessionref = t.sessionregistry
)
-- Step 2: Analyze RGB quality and categorize devices based on their implementation
SELECT 
    makername AS manufacturer,
    modnum AS model,
    devscope AS device_type,
    rgbbright AS rgb_brightness,
    ROUND(rgbcoloracc, 2) AS rgb_color_accuracy,
    rgbzones AS rgb_lighting_zones,
    rgbmodes AS rgb_modes,
    rgbcolors AS supported_colors,
    -- Count number of RGB modes
    ROUND(riq, 2) AS rgb_implementation_quality,
    -- Categorize RGB quality
    CASE 
        WHEN riq > 8.0 THEN 'Premium RGB Implementation'
        WHEN riq BETWEEN 6.0 AND 8.0 THEN 'High-Quality RGB'
        WHEN riq BETWEEN 3.0 AND 5.9 THEN 'Standard RGB'
        ELSE 'Basic RGB'
    END AS rgb_quality_category
FROM rgb_analysis
ORDER BY riq DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/*  Summary of devices per RGB Quality category – count and average RIQ
    Uses GROUPING SETS to add an overall total row                     */

WITH rgb_analysis AS (        -- identical computation of RIQ as before
    SELECT
        r.rgbregistry,
        (r.rgbbright / 100.0) *
        (r.rgbcoloracc / 10.0) *
        (0.5 + r.rgbzones / 20.0)                     AS riq
    FROM rgb r
    JOIN mechanical      m ON r.rgbmechref = m.mechregistry
    JOIN deviceidentity  d ON m.mechdevref = d.devregistry
    JOIN testsessions    t ON d.devsessionref = t.sessionregistry
),
categorized AS (
    SELECT
        riq,
        CASE
            WHEN riq > 8.0               THEN 'Premium RGB Implementation'
            WHEN riq BETWEEN 6.0 AND 8.0 THEN 'High-Quality RGB'
            WHEN riq BETWEEN 3.0 AND 5.9 THEN 'Standard RGB'
            ELSE                              'Basic RGB'
        END AS rgb_quality_category
    FROM rgb_analysis
)
SELECT
    COALESCE(rgb_quality_category, 'TOTAL') AS rgb_quality_category,
    COUNT(*)                                 AS device_count,
    ROUND(AVG(riq), 2)                       AS avg_riq
FROM   categorized
GROUP  BY GROUPING SETS (                    -- one row per category plus a grand total
           (rgb_quality_category),
           ()
       )
ORDER BY GROUPING(rgb_quality_category),     -- put categories first, total last
         avg_riq DESC;

user_query: Analyze customer credit scores by credit classification. Show the credit category, and the customers' details for each category.
sol_sql:
-- Intent: Classify customers into credit score categories and analyze the distribution.
-- Step 1: Use a CASE statement to assign a credit category based on credscore.
-- Step 2: Aggregate counts and average credit scores for each category.
-- Step 3: Order by the average credit score.
SELECT 
  CASE 
    WHEN credscore BETWEEN 300 AND 579 THEN 'Poor'
    WHEN credscore BETWEEN 580 AND 669 THEN 'Fair'
    WHEN credscore BETWEEN 670 AND 739 THEN 'Good'
    WHEN credscore BETWEEN 740 AND 799 THEN 'Very Good'
    WHEN credscore BETWEEN 800 AND 850 THEN 'Excellent'
    ELSE 'Unknown'
  END AS credit_category,
  COUNT(*) AS customer_count,
  ROUND(AVG(credscore), 2) AS average_credscore
FROM credit_and_compliance
GROUP BY credit_category;
follow_up_sol_sql:
-- Intent: Analyze the risk level distribution specifically for customers in the 'Poor' credit score category.
-- Step 1: Filter the credit_and_compliance table to select only records where the credscore falls into the 'Poor' range (300-579).
-- Step 2: Group the filtered results by the risklev column.
-- Step 3: Count the number of customers (*) within each risk level group.
SELECT
  risklev AS risk_level,
  COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM credit_and_compliance
WHERE credscore BETWEEN 300 AND 579 -- Filter for 'Poor' credit score category
GROUP BY risk_level;

user_query: Analyze how lunar interference affects observations by showing the current moon phase, average interference level and the count of problematic events for each observatory, sorted by average interference.
sol_sql:
/*
Intent: Quantify lunar interference impact using LIF calculation
Knowledge Used: LIF (id:9)
Advanced Features: Date functions, lateral join
*/
-- Step 1: Calculate LIF for all observations with lunar data
SELECT 
    o.ObservStation,
    -- Step 2: Calculate average LIF by moon phase
    o.LunarStage,
    AVG((1 - o.LunarDistDeg/180) * (1 - o.AtmosTransparency)) AS avg_lif,
    -- Step 3: Count high interference events
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE (1 - o.LunarDistDeg/180) * (1 - o.AtmosTransparency) > 0.5) AS high_interf_count
FROM Observatories o
GROUP BY o.ObservStation, o.LunarStage
ORDER BY avg_lif DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/*
Intent: Quantify lunar interference impact by switching grouping to geomagnetic status
Knowledge Used: LIF (id:9)
Advanced Features: Date functions, lateral join
*/
-- Step 1: Calculate LIF for all observations with lunar data
SELECT 
    o.ObservStation,
    -- Step 2: Calculate average LIF by geomagnetic status instead of lunar stage
    o.GeomagStatus,
    AVG((1 - o.LunarDistDeg/180) * (1 - o.AtmosTransparency)) AS avg_lif,
    -- Step 3: Count high interference events
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE (1 - o.LunarDistDeg/180) * (1 - o.AtmosTransparency) > 0.5) AS high_interf_count
FROM Observatories o
GROUP BY o.ObservStation, o.GeomagStatus
ORDER BY avg_lif DESC;

user_query: I'd like to see a quality assessment of scans across our archaeological sites. Show site code, site name, scan quality for each site and rank them.
sol_sql:
/*
 * SQL Intent: Calculate the average Scan Quality Score (SQS) for each site to evaluate scan quality.
 * Knowledge Used: #3 (Scan Quality Score - SQS)
 * Advanced Functions: LOG(), POWER(), AVG(), GROUP BY
 */

SELECT
    s.zoneref AS site_code,
    si.zonelabel AS site_name,
    -- Step 1: Calculate the average SQS for each site and round to 2 decimal places
    ROUND(
        AVG(
            POWER(10.0 / (LOG(10, spc.scanresolmm * 1000.0) / LOG(10, spc.pointdense)) * 5.0, 1.5) *
            (spc.coverpct / 100.0) *
            POWER(1.0 - spc.noisedb / 30.0, 2)
        ),
        2
    ) AS avg_sqs
FROM scans s
JOIN scanpointcloud spc ON s.arcref = spc.arcref
JOIN sites si ON s.zoneref = si.zoneregistry
GROUP BY s.zoneref, si.zonelabel
ORDER BY avg_sqs DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/* -----------------------------------------------------------------
 * Add the Environmental Suitability Index (ESI) and classify it with
 * the Environmental Condition Classification System (ECCS) tier
 * for every site, while keeping the previous average SQS ranking.
 * Knowledge used:
 *   #3  – Scan Quality Score (SQS)
 *   #7  – Environmental Suitability Index (ESI)
 *   #50 – Environmental Condition Classification System (ECCS)
 * -----------------------------------------------------------------*/

WITH sqs_per_scan AS (          -- ① SQS for every scan/point-cloud pair
    SELECT
        s.zoneref,
        POWER(
            10.0 /                                  -- 10 / SRI
            ( LOG(10, spc.scanresolmm * 1000.0)
              / LOG(10, spc.pointdense) )            -- SRI denominator
            * 5.0,                                   -- ×5  (see SRI def.)
            1.5                                      -- ^1.5  (SQS weight)
        )
        * (spc.coverpct / 100.0)                     -- SCE term
        * POWER(1.0 - spc.noisedb / 30.0, 2)         -- Noise term
        AS sqs
    FROM scans            s
    JOIN scanpointcloud   spc ON s.arcref = spc.arcref
),
avg_sqs_site AS (            -- ② average SQS per site
    SELECT
        zoneref,
        ROUND(AVG(sqs), 2) AS avg_sqs
    FROM   sqs_per_scan
    GROUP  BY zoneref
),
esi_per_rec AS (             -- ③ ESI for every environment record
    SELECT
        zoneref,
        100
        - 2.5 * ABS(ambictemp - 20)                       -- temperature term
        - POWER(ABS((humepct - 50) / 2), 1.5)             -- humidity term
        - 600.0 / (illumelux + 100)                       -- illumination term
        AS esi
    FROM scanenvironment
),
avg_esi_site AS (            -- ④ average ESI per site
    SELECT
        zoneref,
        ROUND(AVG(esi), 2) AS avg_esi
    FROM   esi_per_rec
    GROUP  BY zoneref
),
site_scores AS (             -- ⑤ merge quality and environment metrics
    SELECT
        s.zoneref,
        s.avg_sqs,
        e.avg_esi
    FROM   avg_sqs_site s
    LEFT   JOIN avg_esi_site e
           ON s.zoneref = e.zoneref
)
SELECT
    ss.zoneref        AS site_code,
    si.zonelabel      AS site_name,
    ss.avg_sqs,
    ss.avg_esi,
    CASE                               /* ECCS tier (#50) */
        WHEN ss.avg_esi >  85 THEN 'Optimal Scanning Conditions'
        WHEN ss.avg_esi >= 70 THEN 'Good Scanning Conditions'
        WHEN ss.avg_esi >= 50 THEN 'Acceptable Scanning Conditions'
        ELSE                           'Challenging Scanning Conditions'
    END            AS environment_tier
FROM   site_scores ss
JOIN   sites       si ON ss.zoneref = si.zoneregistry
ORDER  BY ss.avg_sqs DESC;             -- keep ranking by scan quality

user_query: Group all vendors by their vendor risk level, where for each level, show the level name, the number of entities in that level, and the proportion. Sort the results.
sol_sql:
WITH VendorRisk AS (
    SELECT 
        vm.VendorTrace, 
        CASE 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'A' THEN 4 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'B' THEN 3 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'C' THEN 2 
            ELSE 1 
        END * 
        CASE 
            WHEN vm.ContrState = 'Active' THEN 1 
            ELSE 0.5 
        END AS vri
    FROM VendorManagement vm
),
TieredVendors AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN vri < 2 THEN 'High Risk' 
            WHEN vri < 3 THEN 'Medium Risk' 
            ELSE 'Low Risk' 
        END AS risk_tier
    FROM VendorRisk
)
SELECT 
    risk_tier, 
    COUNT(*) AS vendor_count, 
    ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS percentage
FROM TieredVendors
GROUP BY risk_tier
ORDER BY vendor_count DESC LIMIT 100
follow_up_sol_sql:
WITH VendorRisk AS (
    SELECT 
        vm.VendorTrace, 
        CASE 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'A' THEN 4 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'B' THEN 3 
            WHEN vm.VendSecRate = 'C' THEN 2 
            ELSE 1 
        END * 
        CASE 
            WHEN vm.ContrState = 'Active' THEN 1 
            ELSE 0.5 
        END AS vri
    FROM VendorManagement vm
    WHERE vm.PolComp = 'Non-compliant' OR vm.ProcComp = 'Non-compliant'
),
TieredVendors AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN vri < 2 THEN 'High Risk' 
            WHEN vri < 3 THEN 'Medium Risk' 
            ELSE 'Low Risk' 
        END AS risk_tier
    FROM VendorRisk
)
SELECT 
    risk_tier, 
    COUNT(*) AS vendor_count, 
    ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS percentage
FROM TieredVendors
GROUP BY risk_tier
ORDER BY vendor_count DESC
LIMIT 100;

user_query: Show me the equipment type, equipment code, overall efficiency, safety index, their ranks, and how much those ranks differ across equipment. Sort the results.
sol_sql:
-- Intent: Analyze the relationship between Equipment Efficiency Rating (EER) and safety index
-- This uses knowledge ID 0 and window functions to rank equipment

-- Step 1: Calculate EER for all equipment using the formula from KB
-- EER = (performanceindex + reliabilityindex) / 2 * (1 - environmentalimpactindex / 10)
WITH equipment_efficiency AS (
    SELECT 
        equipmentcode,
        equipmenttype,
        ((performanceindex + reliabilityindex) / 2 * (1 - environmentalimpactindex / 10))::numeric(10,2) AS eer,
        safetyindex
    FROM equipment
)
-- Step 2: Analyze correlation between EER and safety across equipment types
-- Using window functions to rank equipment within their type by EER
SELECT
    equipmenttype,
    equipmentcode,
    eer,
    safetyindex,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY eer DESC) AS efficiency_rank,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY safetyindex DESC) AS safety_rank,
    ABS(RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY eer DESC) - 
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY safetyindex DESC)) AS rank_difference
FROM equipment_efficiency
ORDER BY equipmenttype, eer DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/* ─ Pin-point the biggest EER-vs-Safety outliers per equipment type ─ */
WITH equipment_efficiency AS (               -- ❶ compute EER (KB-0)
    SELECT
        equipmentcode,
        equipmenttype,
        ((performanceindex + reliabilityindex)/2 *
         (1 - environmentalimpactindex/10))::numeric(10,2) AS eer,
        safetyindex
    FROM equipment
),
ranked AS (                                  -- ❷ rank inside each type
    SELECT
        equipmenttype,
        equipmentcode,
        eer,
        safetyindex,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY eer DESC)        AS efficiency_rank,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY safetyindex DESC) AS safety_rank
    FROM equipment_efficiency
),
rank_gaps AS (                               -- ❸ absolute gap per unit
    SELECT
        *,
        ABS(efficiency_rank - safety_rank) AS rank_difference
    FROM ranked
)
SELECT
    equipmenttype,
    equipmentcode,
    eer,
    safetyindex,
    efficiency_rank,
    safety_rank,
    rank_difference
FROM (
    SELECT
        rank_gaps.*,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY equipmenttype ORDER BY rank_difference DESC) AS gap_rank
    FROM rank_gaps
) AS g
WHERE gap_rank = 1                           -- ❹ keep only biggest gap(s)
ORDER BY rank_difference DESC, equipmenttype;

user_query: Can you find gaming controllers that meet high standards? Please show me manufacturer, model, IRS score, joystick precision, drift resistance, trigger resistance, haptic strength, and whether each device meets professional standards.
sol_sql:
/*
Analysis Goal: Identify devices that meet "Professional Esports Controller" standards and their key metrics
Knowledge Used: ID 14 - Professional Esports Controller ID 2
Advanced Features: Conditional expressions, multi-table JOIN, aggregate functions
*/

-- Step 1: Calculate Input Responsiveness Score (IRS) for each controller device
WITH irs_calc AS (
    SELECT 
        d.devregistry,
        d.makername,
        d.modnum,
        -- Calculate IRS score based on formula from knowledge ID 2
        (t.pollratehz / 100.0) * (1 - (t.inplagms + t.resptimems) / 30.0) * 10 AS irs
    FROM deviceidentity d
    JOIN testsessions t ON d.devsessionref = t.sessionregistry
    WHERE t.devscope = 'Controller'
),
-- Step 2: Get controller-related input interaction metrics
controller_metrics AS (
    SELECT 
        i.interactdevref,
        i.joyprec,
        i.driftres,
        i.trigres,
        i.hapstr
    FROM interactionandcontrol i
    JOIN deviceidentity d ON i.interactdevref = d.devregistry
    JOIN testsessions t ON d.devsessionref = t.sessionregistry
    WHERE t.devscope = 'Controller'
)
-- Step 3: Comprehensive analysis and filtering for devices meeting professional esports controller standards
SELECT 
    d.makername AS manufacturer,
    d.modnum AS model,
    ROUND(irs.irs, 2) AS input_responsiveness_score,
    c.joyprec AS joystick_precision,
    c.driftres AS drift_resistance,
    c.trigres AS trigger_resistance,
    c.hapstr AS haptic_strength,
    -- Determine if device meets professional esports controller standards
    CASE 
        WHEN irs.irs > 8.0 AND c.joyprec > 9.0 AND c.driftres > 9.5 
             AND c.trigres >= 5 AND c.hapstr > 8 THEN 'Yes'
        ELSE 'No'
    END AS meets_pro_standard
FROM deviceidentity d
JOIN irs_calc irs ON d.devregistry = irs.devregistry
JOIN controller_metrics c ON d.devregistry = c.interactdevref
ORDER BY meets_pro_standard DESC, input_responsiveness_score DESC;
follow_up_sol_sql:
/*----------------------------------------------
 Goal       :  Per manufacturer, show how many of their controller
              models satisfy the *Professional Esports Controller*
              standard and what share of their catalogue this
              represents.
 Knowledge  :  ID 14 (Professional Esports Controller)  
               ID 2  (Input Responsiveness Score – IRS)
 Features    :  CTEs, conditional expressions, FILTER aggregates,
               percentage calculation with numeric casting
----------------------------------------------*/

WITH irs_calc AS (          -- compute IRS for every controller test-session
    SELECT
        d.devregistry,
        d.makername,
        (t.pollratehz / 100.0)
        * (1 - (t.inplagms + t.resptimems) / 30.0)
        * 10                                             AS irs
    FROM deviceidentity      AS d
    JOIN testsessions        AS t  ON t.sessionregistry = d.devsessionref
    WHERE t.devscope = 'Controller'
),
controller_metrics AS (     -- bring in stick / trigger / haptic metrics
    SELECT
        i.interactdevref                      AS devregistry,
        i.joyprec,
        i.driftres,
        i.trigres,
        i.hapstr
    FROM interactionandcontrol AS i
    JOIN deviceidentity       AS d  ON d.devregistry = i.interactdevref
    JOIN testsessions         AS t  ON t.sessionregistry = d.devsessionref
    WHERE t.devscope = 'Controller'
),
controller_summary AS (     -- flag whether each model meets the pro standard
    SELECT
        d.makername,
        d.devregistry,
        CASE WHEN
             irs.irs   > 8.0 AND
             cm.joyprec   > 9.0 AND
             cm.driftres  > 9.5 AND
             cm.trigres  >= 5   AND
             cm.hapstr    > 8
        THEN 1 ELSE 0 END                       AS is_pro
    FROM deviceidentity      AS d
    JOIN irs_calc            AS irs ON irs.devregistry = d.devregistry
    JOIN controller_metrics  AS cm  ON cm.devregistry  = d.devregistry
)
SELECT
    makername                       AS manufacturer,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_pro = 1)        AS pro_model_count,
    COUNT(*)                                   AS total_controller_models,
    ROUND(
        (COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_pro = 1))::numeric
        / COUNT(*) * 100                      , 1)     AS pro_percentage
FROM controller_summary
GROUP BY makername
ORDER BY pro_percentage DESC, pro_model_count DESC;
